name: Jesus in Japan
description: The 'Jesus in Japan' theory posits that Jesus Christ did not die on the cross but instead fled to Japan, where he lived out his life and died at the age of 106 in the village of Shingō, Aomori Prefecture. This theory emerged in the 1930s with the discovery of the Takeuchi Documents, a controversial set of texts claimed to predate the Bible. Proponents argue that these documents, along with local traditions and burial mounds, provide evidence supporting Jesus's presence in Japan. They claim Jesus studied with a master before journeying to Judea, returned after the crucifixion, and changed his name to Torai Tora Daitenku. The burial mound in Shingō is purported to be Jesus's grave, while another nearby is said to contain a lock of his mother Mary's hair and an ear of his brother's. Mainstream scholarship dismisses the theory as pseudohistory. The Takeuchi Documents are widely regarded as forgeries, and the historical evidence supporting the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus is substantial. The Shingō story is often seen as a local legend developed to promote tourism and capitalize on religious curiosity, lacking credible historical or archaeological support. No credible historical evidence places Jesus in Japan or supports the authenticity of the Takeuchi Documents. The theory is largely based on unsubstantiated claims and reinterpretations of local folklore.
subtitle: Christ's alleged final years in Japan

claims.claim: Jesus did not die on the cross.
claims.evidence: The Takeuchi Documents claim Jesus escaped the crucifixion, with his brother Isukiri taking his place.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historical accounts and biblical texts confirm Jesus's crucifixion. The Takeuchi Documents are considered pseudohistory.

claims.claim: Jesus traveled to Japan and lived in Shingō.
claims.evidence: The existence of 'Jesus's Grave' and 'Mary's Grave' in Shingō, as well as local legends.
claims.criticism: These sites are relatively recent constructs created to attract tourists. There is no archaeological or historical evidence linking Jesus to Shingō.

claims.claim: The Takeuchi Documents are authentic historical records.
claims.evidence: Proponents claim the documents contain details about Jesus's life not found in the Bible.
claims.criticism: Experts consider the Takeuchi Documents to be forgeries created in the 20th century. Their authenticity is widely disputed.

keyFigures.name: Kiyomaro Takeuchi
keyFigures.role: Claimed to have discovered and translated the Takeuchi Documents.

keyFigures.name: Hiroyuki Ehara
keyFigures.role: Japanese spiritual counselor who promoted the Jesus in Japan theory.

keyFigures.name: None (no credible academic proponents)
keyFigures.role: No credible academic historians support this theory

relatedConcepts: Lost Tribes of Israel in Japan

timeline.event: The 'Jesus's Grave' site in Shingō is identified.
The 'Jesus in Japan' theory continues to attract tourists and believers.
